observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
1. the Municipal
Accountant (a) prepare the appropriate journal entry to
revert the amount of bank errors to the CIB account balance and ensure
that only those valid reconciling items are adjusted in the books of
accounts; and (b) make representations with the Management of the
servicing bank to correct the account balance of the Municipality; and
2. the Municipal Treasurer (a) trace and identify the discrepancies and effect
the necessary corrections in the cashbooks and (b) regularly update the
cashbooks based on the bank statements and reconcile the same with
accounting subsidiary records at least every end of the month or as the
need arises, pursuant to Sections 62(F) and 63(F) of the LTOM.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that:
1. the Municipal Treasurer (MT) provide the Municipal Accountant copies
of the updated and current Certified List of All RPT Delinquencies and a
certified list of all real property taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible at the beginning of each year; and
2. the Municipal Accountant (a) prepare the necessary journal entries to
reconcile the balances of the RPT and SET Receivables with the MT’s
Certified List of All RPT Delinquencies; (b) establish the RPT/SET
receivables at the beginning of each year based on the MT’s certified list
of all real property taxpayers; (c) prepare the necessary journal entries to
correct the erroneous recording of income on the RPT delinquencies as of
December 31, 2022; and (d) account for real property taxes using the
Modified Accrual Method as required under Section 19(B) of the MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that:
1. the Municipal Treasurer, in her capacity as the General Services Officer
(GSO), prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant the Summary of
Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) for the previously consumed
inventories amounting to ₱1,214,017.00, and henceforth, observe its
regular preparation and submission pursuant to Section 121 (MNGAS) for
LGUs, Volume 1;
2. the Municipal Accountant derecognize from the books of accounts the
amount of previously consumed inventories based on the SSMI from the
GSO; and
3. the Municipal Accountant and the GSO maintain the Supplies Ledger
Cards and Stock Cards, respectively, as required under Sections 114 and
119 of the same Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (i) make all the necessary
adjustments to the PPE-related accounts in the eNGAS, including the correction of
reported account balances and revisiting the system set-up to omit the residual value
of the Road Networks Account; and (ii) adopt the practice of rendering a detailed
review and analysis of the PPE Lapsing Schedule on a regular basis.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal
Accountant: (i) trace the accounting records to determine the cause/s of the
discrepancies between the Intra-Agency Receivable and Payable accounts and
prepare the necessary journal entries to correct and reconcile the balances of these
accounts; and (ii) henceforth, thoroughly review the accounting entries in the JEVs
to ensure the accuracy of account balances in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal
Accountant:
1. exert efforts in tracing the accounting records to (a) identify the sources
and details of the current balance of the TL-DRRMF account and
henceforth maintain the required subsidiary ledgers therefor and (b)
determine the cause/s of the variance between the balances of the STF bank
account and the TL-DRRMF account and prepare the necessary journal
entries to reconcile the same; and
2. comply with the disclosure requirements of information pertaining to
LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements pursuant to Item VII.2 of
COA Circular No. 2014-002.

We recommended that:
1. the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer (a) immediately
transfer to the Trust Fund (TF) the funds received from the DILG
amounting to ₱5,000,000.00; and (b) henceforth, ensure that funds
received for specific purpose are accrued to the TF pursuant to Section 95
of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1; and
2. the Municipal Accountant (a) observe the recording of appropriate
accounting entries for transactions involving reciprocal accounts to ensure
correctness of the reported balances of all affected accounts in the financial
statements; and (b) submit to the Audit Team copies of the MOA and other
relevant documents on the funds received from NGAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant henceforth, exert diligent
efforts to review and carefully examine the journal entries to ensure the proper
classification of expenditures prior to approval of the Journal Entry Vouchers in
accordance with the prescribed chart of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the timely
submission of complete set of financial statements and supporting schedules within
the prescribed period pursuant to Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001.

We recommended that the:

Municipal Treasurer (i) exert diligent effort in identifying and reconciling the variance of ₱22,663,964.68 with the accounting subsidiary records to support the accuracy of the account balances; and (i) henceforth, regularly reconcile the cashbooks for the CIB-LCCA and CIB-LCSA with accounting records in compliance with Section 63 of the LTOM so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and corrected;

Municipal Mayor (i) designate a Special Disbursing Officer responsible for the disbursement of the fund for financial assistance for individual in crisis situation; (ii) assess the necessity of maintaining PCF depending on the actual operation needs of the municipality; and (iii) if found to be necessary, set up a new PCF sufficient for recurring expenses of the municipality for one month; and

Municipal Accountant (i) reclassify and transfer the current PCF to cash advance account under the accountability of the designated SDO for the disbursement of fund for financial assistance to individuals in crisis situation; (ii) if the establishment of PCF is found to be necessary, estimate the amount of recurring expenses for one month based on historical accounting records of petty expenses of the municipality to be the basis for the initial funding of the PCF; and (iii) regularly review the PCF and assess whether there is need to  increase or decrease the fund.
observations.managementAction: PCF was established and funds for cash assistance are recorded as cash advance.

All discrepancies between physical and book inventories must be investigated, cleared and reconciled immediately. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:



     a) Municipal Treasurer  (i)  prepare  and  maintain  complete  set  of
        cashbooks; (ii)  update  all  cashbooks  and  trace,  identify  and
        reconcile the variance of  ₱85,934,462.98  and  ₱3,928,922.76  with
        accounting records to support the correctness of account  balances;
        (iii) take necessary action immediately for obvious errors or cases
        that  affect  the  accuracy  of  cash  balances;  (iv)  henceforth,
        regularly reconcile the cashbooks for  the  CIB-LCCA  and  CIB-LCSA
        with accounting records in compliance with Section 63 of  the  LTOM
        so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and
        corrected; and



     b) Municipal Accountant (i) immediately furnish the MT copies  of  the
        monthly bank statements upon receipt thereof; (ii)  reclassify  the
        unliquidated cash advances totaling ₱50,000.00 from PCF to Advances
        to Special Disbursing Officers; and (iii) before  the  finalization
        of the financial statements, review all  accounts  to  ensure  that
        their   balances   correctly   represent   the   description    and
        classification of the accounts.

We recommended that the:


     1) Municipal Accountant:


        a) develop accounting estimate for impairment of receivables  based
           on the management’s knowledge and experience on  the  settlement
           of  its  receivables   taking   into   account   the   age   and
           collectability of the balances, identified  bad  debts  eligible
           for   write-off   and   other   historical    data    such    as
           settlement/liquidation and collection experience in the previous
           years;


        b) assess at the end of each year or each  reporting  date  whether
           there is evidence that the receivable accounts are impaired  and
           support the assessment, if possible, with evidence enumerated in
           paragraph 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005  and  submit  to  the
           Municipal Mayor for approval the recommended  list  of  eligible
           accounts for write-off;


        c) calculate the allowance for impairment in  accordance  with  the
           International Public Sector  Accounting  Standards  (IPSAS)  and
           recognize the computed allowance in the books to reflect the net
           realizable value of the receivable  accounts  in  the  financial
           statements;


        d) draw correcting journal entries to revert  the  written-off  SEF
           Tax Receivable balance of ₱1,488,094.00  and  avoid  eliminating
           account balances from the books without legal basis or  required
           authorization; and


        e) reconcile the reciprocal accounts and make  necessary  adjusting
           entries to arrive at equal balances and ensure the  simultaneous
           recording of transaction  to  reciprocal  accounts  to  maintain
           their equal balances and investigate or  trace  any  differences
           for immediate correction and adjustment.

     2) Municipal Mayor:


        a) file a request for write-off of dormant accounts in accordance
           with the guidelines and procedures prescribed  in  COA  Circular
           No. 2016-005, if warranted, but  continue  to  exert  effort  to
           collect the written-off accounts;


        b) develop  policies  and  guidelines  outlining  the  methods  and
           strategies to improve the collection and settlement of past  due
           accounts and  thereafter  ensure  strict  adherence  thereto  to
           cleanse the  books  for  the  fair  presentation  of  receivable
           accounts in the financial statements;


        c) install additional internal  control  to  strictly  monitor  the
           granting and liquidation  of  cash  advances  by  requiring  the
           Accountant’s certification that the employee has no  outstanding
           cash advance before granting a new one and consider imposing the
           “No Liquidation, No Cash Advance Policy”;


        d) ensure that out-going officers/employees  have  no  unliquidated
           cash advances by applying all possible remedies to  enforce  the
           settlement of their account/s such as non-issuance of  clearance
           from cash accountability; and


        e) through the Municipal Accountant, send  demand  letters  to  the
           accountable officers (AOs) with unliquidated cash  advances  and
           cause the withholding of salary of  AOs  who  failed  to  settle
           their cash advances  despite  demand.  Otherwise,  initiate  the
           filing of appropriate administrative charges  against  them,  if
           warranted.

     3) Municipal Treasurer prepare the certified list showing the name  of
        taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to  serve
        as the sole basis of the MA for setting  up  Real  Property/Special
        Education Tax at the beginning of each year.

We recommended that the:


     a) Municipal Mayor (i) provide adequate manpower as support staff that
        will assist the MPMIADC in the conduct of  the  physical  count  of
        MGD’s properties or the MPMIADC create the required  support  staff
        such as requiring all members to assign an available personnel from
        their respective departments who will assist in the  completion  of
        the physical count; and (ii) require the MPMIADC to  perform  their
        duties by conducting the annual physical inventory of PPE,  prepare
        the RPCPPE and  reconcile  it  with  the  property  and  accounting
        records, and submit the RPCPPE to the AT, pursuant to  Section  124
        of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.   All  discrepancies
        between the physical count and property/accounting records must  be
        investigated, cleared and  reconciled  immediately.  If  necessary,
        written  explanation  should  be  required  from  accountable   and
        responsible person. They should likewise ensure that  the  physical
        inventory  is  undertaken  annually,  and  reports  are   submitted
        accordingly; and


     b) the MPMIADC convene and set up  an  effective  schedule  to  manage
        their time  and  carry  out  their  responsibilities  as  committee
        members in overseeing the proper  conduct  of  the  physical  count
        until its completion without  affecting  their  primary  duties  as
        department heads.

We recommended that the:


     a) MDRRMO  or  the  Municipal  Mayor’s  authorized  representative  to
        receive    in-kind    donations    ensure    that    receipt    and
        distribution/utilization  thereof  are  properly   documented   and
        reported pursuant to the accounting and  reporting  guidelines  set
        forth under COA Circular No. 2014-002; and


     b) The Municipal Accountant (i) draw correcting  entries  transferring
        cash  donations  balances  recorded  as  Due  to  NGAs   to   Trust
        Liabilities-DRRMF account and effectively  record  subsequent  cash
        donations  received  for  DRRMF  under  the  proper  account;  (ii)
        determine the nature of funds and cash assistance received  whether
        subject for liquidation and certain conditions, directly recognized
        as income or donation as additional DRRMF to correctly classify and
        record the cash transaction in the books; and  (iii)  disclose  the
        required information regarding DRRM in accordance with Item VII  of
        COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014.

We recommended that the:

     a) BAC, (i) conduct a thorough  planning  for  the  implementation  of
        development projects by clearly identifying the project procurement
        timelines as laid down in the Project Procurement  Management  Plan
        and Annual Procurement Plan;  (ii)  submit  explanation  why  those
        identified  projects  were  not  included   in   the   Annual   and
        Supplemental Procurement Plan;


     b) Municipal Mayor (i) ensure the full or optimum utilization  of  the
        20% DF in accordance with Section 4 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated
        November  4,  2020;  and  (ii)  through  the  Municipal   Engineer,
        supervise and monitor the effective and efficient implementation of
        the projects  under  the  20%  DF  so  that  necessary  and  timely
        intervention can be made to ensure their  completion  in  a  timely
        manner to maximize the developmental benefits for the intended
        beneficiaries;


     c) Municipal Engineer (i) require concerned project engineer, officers
        and employees to be prudent in overseeing the timely implementation
        of projects under their responsibility; (ii)  provide  satisfactory
        explanation as to why the projects were not yet completed,  started
        or implemented; (iii)  and  provide  complete  information  on  the
        status of the projects funded by the 20% DF together with  all  the
        necessary details;


     d) MPDC (i) ensure close coordination with the Municipal Engineer  and
        the   Municipal   Accountant   for   proper   monitoring   of   the
        implementation of the projects funded by the 20% DF and provide all
        the necessary information regarding the status of implementation of
        the projects for the  timely  preparation  and  submission  of  the
        Status Report of Government  PPAs  to  the  Audit  Team;  and  (ii)
        continue to identify projects that are doable and beneficial to the
        constituents; and

     e) MPDC and the Municipal Engineer (i) consider the appropriate timing
        and phasing of the development projects’ implementation taking into
        account the constraints brought about by the still on-going  health
        crisis and set out clearly-defined roles  and  responsibilities  in
        project planning, management  and  monitoring  in  accordance  with
        their  mandate  set  forth  in  RA  No.  7160;  and  (ii)   provide
        satisfactory  justification  for  the  reprogramming   of   various
        projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) gather the necessary
   information from the MPDO/MEO on the  status/accomplishment  of  Dumaran
   Water Supply System to determine  the  amount  of  borrowing  cost  that
   should be capitalized and draw the adjusting journal entries to  reflect
   the correct account balance of  the  qualifying  asset;  and  (b)  fully
   disclose  the  details  or  information  regarding   the   capitalizable
   borrowing costs in the NTFS in accordance with Paragraph 40 of IPSAS No.
   5.

We recommended that  the  Municipal  Mayor  require  the  Municipal
   Engineer and the MPDC to:

     a. comply with the required posting of information  and  publicity  of
        government PPAs in the municipal premises and at the project sites;


     b. provide the Audit Team, at the beginning of the year, with the list
        of all government PPAs to be implemented for a particular year with
        the complete information such as: project name; implementing  unit;
        office or division if it is  not  the  agency  as  a  whole;  brief
        description of the PPAs; contractor or supplier, if  any;  mode  of
        procurement; funding  source;  cost  or  approved  budget;  project
        duration including start and completion dates; and location;


     c. inform the AT within ten days after the award of  the  projects  or
        before  the  start  of  the  PPAs  that  the  appropriate   project
        signboards and/or public notices are already posted at  the  agency
        premises and at the project sites; and


     d. submit monthly monitoring  report  on  the  implementation  of  all
        Municipality’s PPAs in accordance with COA  Circular  No.  2013-004
        and ensure that the cost incurred to date indicated in  the  report
        reconcile with the records of the MA.

We recommended that the MGD, through the GFPS:


      a) continue in promoting and ensuring gender  mainstreaming  in  local
         policy-making, planning,  programming,  budgeting,  implementation,
         monitoring and evaluation;

      b) strategically plan the  implementation  of  GAD  PPAs  to  counter,
         navigate or narrow down unavoidable and existing challenges that
         will affect its implementation;

      c) consider “what-if” scenarios during  the  assessment  and  planning
         phase of the formulation of the GPB to  come  up  with  alternative
         strategies and “plan B” to help the MGD  to  be  more  prepared  to
         potential threats and minimize its risk to be able to continue  and
         ensure the 100% implementation of its GAD PPAs; and

      d) evaluate  the  existing  GAD  Code  and  amend  or  revise  it,  if
         necessary.   Henceforth,   periodically   review,   evaluate    and
         recalibrate, as necessary, its GAD Code to keep up with the changes
         and development in addressing gender  issues  to  ensure  that  the
         outcome of MGD’s GAD system remains in line with the government  in
         achieving its GAD objectives.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  scrutinize  the
    records on  taxes  and  mandatory  salary  deductions  and  ensure  that
    adjustments and corrections of balances are supported with  valid  basis
    and are justified. Henceforth, keep the books organized by (i) following
    the basic accounting principles and procedures;  (ii)  track  errors  as
    soon as possible; and (iii) regularly review and update the records.

We recommended that the:

a) Municipal Mayor (i) stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to officers and employees who still have unliquidated balances; (ii) install additional internal control to strictly monitor the granting of cash advances by requiring the Accountant’s  certification that the employee has no outstanding cash advance before granting a new one and consider imposing the “No Liquidation, No Cash Advance Policy”; (iii) ensure that out-going officers/employees have no unliquidated cash advances by applying all possible remedies to enforce the settlement of their account/s such as non-issuance of clearance from cash accountability; and (iv) stop granting cash advances to non-employees of the Municipality. In case of cash advances for operating expenses, e.g., utilization of funds for programs and activities under the SEF, designate a municipal employee as Special Disbursing Officer to handle the disbursement;

b) Municipal Accountant (i) cause the withholding of salary of the AOs who failed to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period; (ii) exert effort in retrieving prior years’ accounting records relative to the unaccounted balance of ₱456,023.67 and identify the nature or purpose of the ₱17,482,181.93 to establish the validity of the claims against the concerned accountable officials; (iii) trace the accounting records of the negative balances and make necessary correcting entries for verified errors by reversing the entries for excess liquidation and transfer the excess refund to the liability account; and (iv) monitor the records for granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to minimize errors in the accounting of cash advances; and

c) Municipal Administrator, in coordination with other officers and employees concerned, exhaust all possible remedies to collect/settle the receivables by regularly serving written notice to concerned accountable officers and employees to demand settlement of the outstanding cash advances and institute the necessary legal action against those who failed to settle their accountabilities. For dormant accounts, request for write-off pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 but continue to exert effort to collect or settle the involved accounts.
observations.reason:
Cash advances were still granted to Special Disbursing Officers with unliquidated accounts

Demand letters were issued to erring officials but the Municipal Accountant failed to withhold the salaries of those who failed to settled their accounts; and

Negative and unaccounted balances are still existing.

No request for write-off was initiated

This is reiterated in Observation No. 2 in this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

Municipal Mayor (i) require the Inventory Committee to conduct the annual physical inventory of PPE, reconcile the results of the count with the property and accounting records, and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.  All discrepancies between physical and book inventories must be investigated, cleared and reconciled immediately. personnel, procurement of the aforementioned lacking standby/stock of food packs
and hygiene kits, medical supplies, equipment and tools, and construction of
warehouse to comply with the minimum standards on disaster preparedness.

open burning of wastes and its prohibition; and

sanitary landfill meeting the requirements of Sections 40, 41 and 42 of RA
No. 9003.

System (GFPS): a) ensure that at least 5% of the total budget is allocated to GAD
PPAs; and b) closely monitor the implementation of PPAs to achieve optimal

No. 2013-01.

Treasurer strictly adhere to the instructions contained in the COE to withhold any
amount due to all concerned persons liable, otherwise, the Audit Team will be
compelled to report the matter through the Regional Director, to the General
Counsel for further legal action.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor serve a written notice to accountable officers (AOs) and demand settlement of the outstanding cash advances and other receivables. In all instances, the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant must ensure that the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 are strictly observed and that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that 1) the Municipal Budget Officer verify the balances of appropriations in the Registries of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAOs) before certifying in the Obligation Requests as to existence of available appropriation and immediately record in the RAAOs any changes in the Annual Budget made through Augmentation Ordinances and Supplemental Budgets to ensure that declared “savings” are deducted from the original appropriations; 2) the Municipal Accountant maintain records on allotment and verify its balances before certifying in the Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) as to obligation of allotment; 3) the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records on allotments and obligations monthly so that resulting discrepancies can be immediately verified and corrected and required reports can be immediately submitted and 4) the Municipal Mayor implement the “allotment system” as it is an important budget control tool to ensure that obligations incurred will not exceed authorized appropriations.
observations.managementAction: No managemen t reply

observations.recommendation:
We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor seek the commitment of the provincial government in implementing the alliance projects in accordance with the agreed plans and schedules to ensure optimum utilization of funds and effective and efficient provision of basic services and facilities to constituents.
The municipal government should also obtain original copies of the Schedules forming part of the MOA as approved by the Sangguniang Bayan and of the feasibility studies, business plans and detailed engineering documents relative to the alliance projects and submit authenticated copies of the same to this Office as reference in audit.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended for  the  Municipal  Mayor  to  require  the  Inventory
Committee to  reconcile  the  results  of  the  physical  count,  which  was
conducted on August 2016, with  the  property  and  accounting  records  and
submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor pursuant to Section 124 of  the  Manual  on
NGAS for LGUs,  Volume I.  All  discrepancies  between  physical  and  book
inventories must be investigated, cleared  and  reconciled  immediately.  If
necessary, written explanation  should  be  required  from  accountable  and
responsible person. He should likewise ensure  that  physical  inventory  be
undertaken annually and reports be submitted accordingly.

In  addition,  the  OIC-Municipal  Accountant  and  the  OIC-Municipal
Treasurer should be required to reconstruct and thereafter maintain the  PPE
ledger cards and property cards in the form prescribed in the  said  manual.
Also, the OIC-Municipal Treasurer should ensure  that  all  issued  PPE  for
official use of officials and employees are covered by ARE  which  shall  be
renewed every January of third year after issue.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to serve a  written  notice  to
accountable officers and demand settlement of the outstanding cash  advances
and other  receivables.  Moreover,  we  recommended  for  the  OIC-Municipal
Accountant to locate  and  submit  to  the  auditor  the  vouchers  and  all
supporting  documents  relevant  to  the  grant  of  the  unliquidated  cash
advances to facilitate  the  filing  of  appropriate  cases  against  erring
accountable officers.

In all instances, the Municipal Mayor and the OIC-Municipal Accountant
must ensure that each provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002  dated  February
10, 1997 are  strictly  observed  and  that  all  cash  advances  are  fully
liquidated at the end of each year.

Grant of cash  advances  to  individuals  who  were  not  officers  or
employees of the Municipal  Government  must  likewise  be  stopped  as  the
Municipal Mayor  has  no  administrative  supervision  over  them  and  thus
withholding of salaries and appropriate  administrative  proceedings  cannot
be instituted against such individuals with unliquidated cash advances.

The OIC-Municipal Accountant should also coordinate  with  the  former
Municipal Accountant to itemize the unidentified balances and to review  the
negative balances so that proper adjusting entries may be taken  up  in  the
books.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to update her  cashbook
and regularly reconcile  the  same  with  the  accounting  records  so  that
discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and  corrected.  She
should also immediately deposit her collections  intact,  as  prescribed  in
Section 62 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual to  avoid  possible  loss
or misapplication of funds.

Moreover,  considering  the  balances  of  accountability  of   former
Municipal Treasurers, we recommended that additional personnel  be  assigned
to the Office  of  the  Municipal  Accountant  so  that  one  can  focus  on
reconciling the records of former  Municipal  Treasurers  to determine  the
details of their accountabilities in order that appropriate  action  may  be
undertaken for its settlement.

The Municipal Mayor should henceforth monitor and see to it  that  the
provisions of the  Local  Treasury  Operations  Manual  on  the  remittance,
verification  and  deposit  of  collections,  preparation   of   Report   of
Collections and Deposits and Report of Accountability for Accountable  Forms
and reconciliation of cashbooks and accounting records are strictly  adhered
to by the incumbent Municipal Treasurer  and  collectors  to  avoid  similar
accumulation of Cash – Local Treasury account balance.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the  budget  for
the ensuing years are prepared in accordance with the procedures,  schedules
and local budget preparation forms prescribed under  the  Budget  Operations
Manual for LGUs and for the Sangguniang  Bayan  to  require,  prior  to  the
approval of the  Annual  and  Supplemental  Budgets,  that  special  purpose
appropriations are categorized by object of expenditure and presented  in  a
similar sequence as a department or office expenditure  program  except  the
staffing and compensation profile where there are no  personnel  and  office
to operate.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare and  submit
the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts  for  CY  2016  and
ensure  that  material  variances/differences  between  budget  and   actual
amounts are properly reviewed, reconciled and adequately  disclosed  in  the
Notes to the Financial Statements.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to  ensure  that  the  required
reports on PPAs are submitted within the period prescribed in  COA  Circular
No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 as amended by COA Circular No.  2015-006
dated August 20, 2015. The monitoring  report  on  the  Municipality’s  PPAs
should contain details as  to  project  name,  location,  total  cost,  date
started, extensions, target and actual completion date, project  status  and
total cost incurred to date duly reconciled with the accounting records.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the BAC and BAC Secretariat to submit the  contract
and supporting documents  for  the  “Water  Source  Development  Project  at
Nabancao, Bgy. Sta. Teresita, Dumaran” amounting to ₱2,889,554.48.

Henceforth,  the  Municipal  Mayor  should  enjoin  the  BAC  and  BAC
Secretariat  for  the  timely  submission  of copies of government contracts  and
each of all the documents forming part thereof within five (5) working  days
from its execution, pursuant to Sections 3.1.1 and  3.1.2  of  COA  Circular
No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to ensure timely review and  evaluation
of the same  so  that  management  could  be  immediately  informed  of  any
defects/deficiencies noted thereon.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for  the  Municipal  Mayor  to  ensure  that  the  OIC-
Municipal Accountant submits the financial statements,  journals,  and
supporting schedules and documents  within  the  period  prescribed  in  the
Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the SWM Board to hasten the preparation of the 10-
year SWM Plan for 2016-2025 and the LGU to ensure the establishment of  MRF
in every barangay, in accordance with R.A. No. 9003  and  its  implementing
rules and regulations for the furtherance of the objectives of the Act.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor  instruct  the  ICO-Municipal
Treasurer to immediately transfer to  a  special  trust  fund  the  CY  2016
unexpended balance of the QRF and DRRM Fund -  MOOE  totaling  ₱2,063,441.76
and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to record  said  transfer  in  the  account
“Trust Liabilities-DRRM” under the trust fund books, pursuant to Section  21
of R.A. No. 10121 and Section 5.1.10 of  COA  Circular  No.  2012-002  dated
September 12, 2012.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for  the  Municipal  Mayor  to  require  the  Municipal
Development Council (MDC) during the preparation of  the  annual  budget  to
include only developmental PPAs which are feasible,  specific  and  properly
identified as to target  date  of  execution  and  the  personnel  in-charge
responsible for the implementation. The MDC should also coordinate with  the
Municipal Engineer so that PPAs are  supported  with  Program  of  Works  to
ensure  optimum  fund  utilization  and   timely   and   efficient   project
implementation.

The Local Finance Committee should review the balances  of  continuing
appropriations for every end of the  year  for  possible  inclusion  in  the
preparation of the annual budget. Unexpended balances of appropriations  for
PPAs which were already  completed/accomplished  should  be  re-appropriated
for other projects of the ensuing year.

Lastly, the Municipal Planning and Development  Coordinator,  together
with the MDC, should coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation  of
the different development PPAs of the Municipality in accordance  with  the
approved development plan and properly render  a  report  to  the  Municipal
Mayor the status of every project, problems encountered and  recommendations
to address such problem.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the members of the GFPS to strictly adhere with the
provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC  No.  2013-01  in  the  preparation  and
allocation of GPB. The GFPS should also ensure that all  recommendations  of
the DILG Regional Office in the review of the GPB are properly observed
before incorporating the same in the Annual Budget.

Lastly, proper training and  seminar  should  be  intensified  to  the
members of the GFPS relative  to  GAD  concepts,  principles  and  policies,
especially on GAD planning  and  budgeting  to enhance  and  broaden  their
knowledge and proper execution of GAD PPAs.

The Audit Team did  not  receive  any  response  from  the  management
relative to the above audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to require the concerned municipal government officials to immediately submit the required reports for 2015 and to henceforth strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 particularly on the requirements in the release and use of LDRRMF to avoid suspension and/or disallowance in audit.

We also recommended for the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to ensure that the Annual GAD Plan and Budget was approved by the DILG before incorporating the same in the Annual Budget. The audit team should also be furnished with the GPB and AR as references in audit.
